mission.title: Install and Configure Drasi on Kubernetes
mission.description: Drasi is a data processing platform that simplifies detecting changes in data and taking immediate action. It provides real-time actionable insights without the overhead of traditional data processing methods, making it ideal for applications that require immediate responses to data changes.

steps.title: Clone the Drasi repository
steps.description:
Clone the Drasi repository to your local machine to access the necessary Kubernetes manifests.
```bash
git clone https://github.com/drasi-project/drasi-platform.git
cd drasi-platform
```

steps.title: Apply the Kubernetes manifests
steps.description:
Deploy Drasi to your Kubernetes cluster using the provided manifests.
```bash
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml --namespace drasi --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the deployment
steps.description:
Check if the Drasi pods are running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace drasi
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
Configure resource limits for the Drasi deployment to ensure optimal performance.
```bash
kubectl set resources deployment/drasi --namespace drasi --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi --requests=cpu=250m,memory=256Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC for Drasi
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding to allow Drasi to access necessary resources.
```bash
kubectl apply -f k8s/rbac.yaml --namespace drasi
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Drasi deployment
uninstall.description:
Delete the Drasi deployment from your Kubernetes cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete deployment drasi --namespace drasi
```

uninstall.title: Clean up associated resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any associated resources such as services and config maps.
```bash
kubectl delete service drasi --namespace drasi
kubectl delete configmap drasi-config --namespace drasi
```

uninstall.title: Delete the namespace
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the namespace to clean up all resources.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace drasi
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Export the current configuration and state of Drasi before upgrading.
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace drasi -o yaml > drasi-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the manifests
upgrade.description:
Pull the latest changes from the Drasi repository to get the updated manifests.
```bash
git pull origin main
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the deployment
upgrade.description:
Apply the updated manifests to upgrade Drasi.
```bash
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml --namespace drasi
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the pods to ensure the upgrade was successful.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace drasi
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Drasi pods are not starting, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace drasi
``` 
Look for any error messages that indicate what might be wrong, such as configuration issues or missing dependencies.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being terminated due to OOMKilled, check the resource usage:
```bash
kubectl top pods --namespace drasi
``` 
Consider increasing the memory limits in your deployment.

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permission errors
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission errors, verify the RBAC configuration:
```bash
kubectl get rolebindings --namespace drasi
kubectl describe rolebinding <rolebinding-name> --namespace drasi
``` 
Ensure that the correct permissions are granted.

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Drasi will have all pods running without errors, and you will be able to access the service as intended. You can verify this by checking the pod status and ensuring that the application is responding to data changes.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl configured to interact with it.
